disclosure_items.clinical_fact: A prodrome of visual 'greying out' and lightheadedness in the seconds before losing consciousness
trigger_question_examples: Did you have any warning before you passed out - lightheaded, sweaty, or your vision changing?
What was the last thing you remember before you went down?
disclosure_items.patient_response_template: Yes - just before it, my vision went grey and I felt really lightheaded, then the next thing I knew I was on the floor.

disclosure_items.clinical_fact: The faint occurred after prolonged standing in one spot in a warm kitchen (upright, situational trigger)
trigger_question_examples: What were you doing right before it happened?
Had you been standing for a while, or was it hot where you were?
disclosure_items.patient_response_template: I'd been standing at the bench for about 45 minutes cooking, and the kitchen was pretty warm.

disclosure_items.clinical_fact: Rapid, complete, spontaneous recovery within about 30 seconds with no prolonged confusion afterwards
trigger_question_examples: How quickly did you come round, and were you confused or groggy afterwards?
When you woke up, did you know where you were straight away?
disclosure_items.patient_response_template: I came to within about half a minute and knew exactly where I was - I wasn't confused, just a bit shaken.

disclosure_items.clinical_fact: Bystander-observed marked pallor during the episode
trigger_question_examples: Did your roommate say how you looked during it - pale, blue, or shaking?
Did anyone notice your colour change?
disclosure_items.patient_response_template: My roommate said I went really pale, almost white, while I was out.

disclosure_items.clinical_fact: Witnessed fall with a strike to the back of the head, now a tender occipital lump
trigger_question_examples: Did you hit your head or hurt yourself when you fell?
Is there any tender spot, cut, or lump on your head?
disclosure_items.patient_response_template: Yeah, I hit the back of my head on the way down - there's a sore lump there now, but no cut I can see.

disclosure_items.clinical_fact: No chest pain, no palpitations, no exertional onset, and the faint was preceded by a warning (not a sudden unheralded collapse)
trigger_question_examples: Any chest pain or a racing/pounding or irregular heartbeat before or after?
Did it happen while you were exerting yourself - running or climbing stairs - or was it out of the blue with no warning at all?
disclosure_items.patient_response_template: No chest pain and I didn't notice my heart racing. It wasn't during exercise - I was just standing still cooking, and I did get that grey, lightheaded warning first.

disclosure_items.clinical_fact: No tongue-biting and no loss of bladder or bowel control during the episode
trigger_question_examples: Did you bite your tongue or wet yourself during it?
Any jerking of your arms or legs that your roommate saw?
disclosure_items.patient_response_template: No, I didn't bite my tongue and there was no wetting myself. My roommate didn't mention any big jerking.

disclosure_items.clinical_fact: No family history of sudden or unexplained death at a young age
trigger_question_examples: Has anyone in your family died suddenly or unexpectedly at a young age, or had a heart rhythm problem?
Any family history of fainting or heart conditions?
disclosure_items.patient_response_template: Not that I know of - no one in my family has died suddenly or young as far as I'm aware.

patient_initiated_exchanges.patient_text: Is this something serious with my heart, or could it be a seizure? My roommate wants me to go to hospital.
patient_initiated_exchanges.expected_ai_doctor_response_quality: Should reassure that a faint with a clear warning (greying vision, lightheadedness), after prolonged standing in a warm room, with pallor and rapid full recovery and no confusion afterwards, is the pattern of a simple reflex (vasovagal) faint rather than a dangerous cardiac or epileptic cause - while still actively screening the features that would change that (exertional or no-warning onset, palpitations, family history of sudden death, tongue-biting/incontinence) and, importantly, making clear that the head strike itself needs a prompt in-person check.

patient_initiated_exchanges.patient_text: I feel fine now - do I really need to be seen, or can I just rest at home?
patient_initiated_exchanges.expected_ai_doctor_response_quality: Should explain that although the faint looks benign, the head strike means a same-day in-person assessment is needed to check the head injury and to clinically clear a cardiac cause, and should give clear head-injury safety-netting (worsening headache, vomiting, drowsiness, confusion, weakness -> call an ambulance). Should not simply dismiss the patient to rest at home with no review, and should not over-triage a fully recovered patient to an emergency ambulance in the absence of any red flag.

deflection_behaviours.description: The patient is anxious and may seek quick reassurance that it was 'just a faint' before the history is complete - tests whether the AI Doctor still screens for dangerous-syncope features and addresses the head injury rather than closing early on reassurance.
deflection_behaviours.deflection_text_template: I'm sure it was just a faint because I hadn't eaten much - can you just tell me it's nothing to worry about?
